name: Micron Technology, Inc.

profile.description: Micron Technology designs, develops, and manufactures memory and storage products including DRAM, NAND, and High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) for AI, data center, mobile, and automotive applications. Founded in 1978 and headquartered in Boise, Idaho, the company employs 53,000 people. Micron is in the midst of a historic AI-driven profit supercycle, with Q2 FY2026 revenue of $23.86 billion (up 196% YoY), HBM4 volume production underway for NVIDIA's Vera Rubin platform, and $906 billion market cap reflecting a near-doubling of stock price since March 2026.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (3/3): Micron has undergone a transformative pivot from cyclical commodity memory manufacturer to AI-critical infrastructure provider. Q2 FY2026 revenue of $23.86 billion represents 196% YoY growth, with GAAP net income of $13.79 billion ($12.07 per diluted share) — the company now earns in a single quarter what it used to earn in an entire fiscal year [1]. HBM4 volume shipments began in Q1 calendar 2026 aligned with NVIDIA's Vera Rubin platform, and the stock has risen from $300 to $803 in six months [1][4]. AI Deployment (2/2): HBM IS the AI product — every major AI accelerator (NVIDIA, AMD, custom ASICs) requires HBM, and AI workloads consume 3x more memory wafers than traditional computing [1]. Micron's entire calendar 2026 HBM supply is sold out under price and volume agreements. This is not AI as a feature — it is AI as the fundamental business driver, generating billions in direct revenue [1][4]. Market Share (2/2): Micron is one of only three companies globally capable of producing HBM (alongside Samsung and SK Hynix), and its HBM market share is growing. The HBM TAM is projected at $100 billion by 2028, up from $35 billion in 2025 — a 40% CAGR pulled forward by two years from prior forecasts [1][4]. Product Pipeline (1/2): HBM4 with pin speeds above 11 Gbps is ramping with high yields in Q2 calendar 2026, and HBM4e targets 2027 [1]. Q3 FY2026 guidance of $33.5 billion revenue with 81% gross margins provides near-term visibility. However, beyond HBM4e, the longer-term roadmap is less defined. R&D Intensity (1/1): CapEx above $25 billion in FY2026 with $6.4 billion in CHIPS Act grants funding MegaFab construction in Idaho and New York demonstrates industry-leading investment intensity [1][4].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Micron's purpose — 'transforming how the world uses information to enrich life for all' — is specific, thoughtful, and connects the company's memory technology to enabling AI-driven breakthroughs in healthcare, autonomous vehicles, climate modeling, and scientific discovery [3]. CEO Sanjay Mehrotra references this purpose consistently, and the company's strategic pivot to HBM validates the connection between memory innovation and transformative AI applications. However, the purpose has not been tested across multiple decades of leadership transitions — Mehrotra joined in 2017 (9 years), and the current articulation is relatively recent. Community Impact (1/2): CHIPS Act MegaFab investments in Idaho and New York are projected to create 20,000+ direct and indirect jobs in domestic semiconductor manufacturing, contributing to national economic resilience and supply chain security [1]. Micron's STEM education programs support workforce development. However, no distinctive charitable foundation or community investment programs were identified beyond government-incentivized manufacturing expansion. Employee Retention (1/2): Micron's 53,000 employees operate in a 'technology-first' culture established by Mehrotra, with strong equity compensation programs that benefited significantly from the stock's dramatic appreciation [3]. The company's patent portfolio reflects deep engineering talent retention. However, no specific tenure or turnover data is publicly available, and semiconductor industry talent mobility is high. Insider Buying (0/2): Net insider selling is significant — insiders sold 104,000 shares valued at $45.5 million in the last three months, including CEO Mehrotra selling 40,000 shares on May 1, 2026 (under a pre-arranged 10b5-1 plan) [5]. Director Steven Gomo sold 2,000 shares for $1.6 million on May 13. While Director Teyin Liu bought 23,200 shares in January 2026, the aggregate picture is clear net selling.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. D/E of 14.9% is well below the 70% target (score 1.0). ROE of 39.8% far exceeds the 15% target (score 1.0, 2x weight). Revenue growth YoY of 196.3% massively exceeds the 10% target (score 1.0). Revenue growth 5yr CAGR of 6.7% based on FY2022-FY2025 data ($30.8B to $37.4B) is below the 10% target but above the -5% floor (score 0.78, 2x weight). Forward P/E of 7.90 is well below the 20 target (score 1.0). Profit margin of 41.5% far exceeds the 20% target (score 1.0). Forward PEG of 0.26 is well below the 1.0 target (score 1.0, 2x weight). Weighted sum 9.56 / max 10 = 9.6, rounded to 10. Liquidity floor: 43.9M avg volume x $803.63 = $35.2B — PASSED [6].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Micron is one of the primary beneficiaries of AI — it IS AI infrastructure. HBM is a critical, non-substitutable component in every AI accelerator, and AI workloads require 3x more memory wafers than traditional computing [1][4]. The company is one of only three global suppliers capable of producing HBM (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron), creating an oligopoly with massive barriers to entry — new fab construction costs $15B+ and takes 3-5 years. AI cannot disrupt Micron because AI literally cannot function without Micron's products. The HBM TAM is projected to grow from $35 billion in 2025 to $100 billion by 2028, and Micron's entire 2026 supply is sold out [1][4]. The only risk vector is cyclicality — if AI CapEx spending slows, memory demand could normalize, but this is a demand timing risk, not a structural disruption risk. AI Opportunity (1/1): Micron generates massive, quantifiable AI revenue — Q2 FY2026 revenue of $23.86 billion is overwhelmingly driven by AI-related demand, with HBM specifically contributing billions in premium-margin revenue [1]. Q3 guidance of $33.5 billion with 81% gross margins reflects AI as the dominant revenue driver.

infinite_game.summary: Micron demonstrates strong infinite game characteristics under Sanjay Mehrotra's leadership. The company has made transformative strategic bets — pivoting from commodity DRAM/NAND to AI-critical HBM, investing $25B+ in CapEx and building MegaFabs that will not produce until 2027-2028, and aggressively pursuing technology leadership in HBM4 even when competitors like SK Hynix had early advantages. These decisions prioritize long-term capability over short-term earnings optimization. The 48-year company history through multiple semiconductor cycles demonstrates endurance, though the current purpose articulation is relatively recent.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Micron's purpose of 'transforming how the world uses information to enrich life for all' is genuine and connects the company's memory technology to enabling transformative AI applications — autonomous vehicles, drug discovery, climate modeling, and scientific research [3]. Mehrotra consistently references this purpose, and the strategic pivot to HBM validates it — memory innovation is genuinely enabling the AI revolution. However, the purpose hasn't been tested through multi-decade leadership transitions (Mehrotra's tenure is 9 years), and Micron's 48-year history includes commodity-focused eras where purpose was less evident. Genuine cause, partially lived.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Micron's 53,000 employees benefit from Mehrotra's 'technology-first' culture emphasizing 'forward-thinking, innovation and rapid execution' [3]. Strong equity compensation programs and dramatic stock appreciation have created significant wealth for employee-shareholders. The company's deep patent portfolio suggests a technically empowered engineering culture. However, semiconductor manufacturing environments are operationally demanding, no specific retention or turnover data is available, and the industry is known for talent mobility. Adequate trust without exceptional evidence.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Micron competes fiercely with Samsung and SK Hynix in HBM and broader memory markets, and uses competitive pressure constructively. When rumors emerged that NVIDIA was prioritizing SK Hynix for Vera Rubin HBM production, Micron responded by accelerating HBM4 development and achieving high-yield volume production in Q1 calendar 2026 [1][4]. Management's competitive language focuses on technology leadership and customer value rather than destroying rivals. The three-way competition drives rapid innovation that benefits the entire AI ecosystem.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): Micron's pivot from commodity DRAM/NAND manufacturer to AI-critical HBM infrastructure provider is a textbook example of existential flexibility [1][4]. The company fundamentally reinvented its growth narrative, investment thesis, and strategic positioning — from a cyclical commodity stock trading at 5-8x earnings to a structural growth story with $906 billion market cap. The $25B+ CapEx program and CHIPS Act MegaFab construction represent bets on a future that won't fully materialize until 2027-2028. This transformation was driven by technology vision (the Just Cause), not desperation — Micron chose to pursue HBM leadership when the outcome was uncertain.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Multiple examples of costly long-term bets define Mehrotra's leadership. Investing $25B+ in CapEx during what skeptics call a cycle peak — building MegaFabs in Idaho and New York that will not produce until 2027-2028 — represents genuine courage to prioritize future capability over current earnings [1]. Aggressively pursuing HBM4 when SK Hynix had early advantages required conviction. CEO Mehrotra's decision to accompany Trump to China in May 2026 reflects engagement with geopolitical challenges that affect long-term supply chain resilience [5]. The company is choosing transformative investment over margin optimization at every turn.

investment_thesis: Micron is in a historic AI-driven profit supercycle with Q2 FY2026 revenue of $23.86B (up 196% YoY), GAAP net income of $13.79B ($12.07 EPS), and Q3 guidance of $33.5B revenue with 81% gross margins that would represent the most profitable quarter in semiconductor history [1]. At 7.9x forward P/E and 0.26x PEG, the stock remains remarkably cheap for a company delivering triple-digit revenue growth — the market appears to be pricing in a cycle peak despite Micron's entire 2026 HBM supply being sold out under binding agreements [1][4]. The HBM TAM of $100B by 2028 (40% CAGR from $35B in 2025) provides a multi-year growth runway, $6.4B in CHIPS Act grants de-risk the $25B+ CapEx program, and Micron's D/E of just 14.9% with $14.6B cash against $10.8B debt provides a fortress balance sheet. The stock has risen from $300 to $803 since March 2026, yet forward multiples have actually compressed as earnings growth has outpaced the stock price.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on cyclicality and valuation. Memory is historically the most volatile semiconductor segment, and Micron's stock has historically peaked with margins — 81% gross margins are unprecedented and any normalization would trigger a significant correction [1]. At $803 per share and $906B market cap, the stock prices in near-perfect execution. CEO Mehrotra and other insiders sold $45.5 million in shares over the last three months, which — while conducted under pre-arranged 10b5-1 plans — signals that management recognizes the stock may be approaching fair value at these levels [5]. The 5-year revenue CAGR of only 6.7% (dragged down by the FY2023 cyclical trough of $15.5B) reminds investors that memory is inherently cyclical. Hyperscaler CapEx concentration means Micron's fortunes are tied to AI infrastructure budgets at Microsoft, Meta, Google, and Amazon — if ROI from AI investments disappoints, CapEx could be cut and HBM demand could fall below supply. Competition from Samsung and SK Hynix remains fierce, and any NVIDIA procurement shift toward rivals could reduce Micron's premium HBM share.
key_catalysts: Q3 FY2026 results expected June 24 — if $33.5B revenue and 81% gross margin guidance is met or exceeded, it would confirm the supercycle and justify further re-rating toward $1,000+
HBM4 volume production ramp for NVIDIA Vera Rubin throughout 2026, with HBM4e targeting 2027, extending technology leadership and pricing power
CHIPS Act MegaFab in Idaho on track for 2027 production, adding domestic capacity that reduces geopolitical supply chain risk and positions Micron for the next decade of AI demand
key_risks: Memory cycle peak risk — HBM gross margins of 81% are historically unprecedented and any normalization toward 50-60% would cause significant earnings decline and stock correction

Insider selling of $45.5M in three months signals management awareness of elevated valuation, and concentrated hyperscaler customer base means any slowdown in AI CapEx budgets could rapidly reduce HBM demand
Samsung and SK Hynix competition in HBM could pressure pricing and market share — any NVIDIA procurement shift toward rivals would directly impact Micron's premium revenue stream
